story:
Outlaws burst from the black wood, drunk and fearless, beating their painted chests as armored dogs churned the mud at their heels. At the mob’s heart rode their knight: black-bearded, braided like a barbarian, nose pierced, eyes rimmed with soot. His armor was a dented patchwork; a flensed bear pelt crowned his bald head. A wanderer, he gathered strays, living on raid and fire, serving no lord but appetite.

Through mist they rushed a grim tower where gargoyles drooled gutter water. A crude battering ram, tattooed with misspelled boasts, smashed the portcullis. The men flooded the gatehouse and fanned through cobwebbed halls. They climbed the spiral to the hatch. Empty. 

Outside, the knight rasped, “Where is it?” One-eyed Marcel warned the boys wanted loot, not air. The knight ordered wine and a blaze: spectacle to keep blood hot, time to keep faith.

He turned to a small chapel barred with rotted planks. One blow opened it. At the altar—one black slab etched with a dead tree—sat a polished stag skull, antlers branching like ivory veins. Behind a padlocked door lay a cloister with grass and fruit. He broke the lock and found two twisting pomegranate trees shading a spring greened by jade soil.

There floated the lady of the spring, hair spilled like honey, fingers drawing circles on the water. Golden inked eyes stared from her belly; her own eyes were milk-white, reflecting everything. “You’ve come,” she said, voice full of water. “There is no resisting temptation. Take what you came for.”

He planted his axe on shore and waded in. Brutal by habit and drunk on legend, he tried to claim her by right of conquest, boasting that his seed would breed a brood of horned and winged horrors to ravage the land. It was a lie he told himself: that violence makes law. She struggled, then asked softly, “Do you like monsters?” In her blank gaze he saw himself—the missing pelt, the soot streaming, the ripples racing behind him.

The water thickened. A banded, golden body uncoiled beneath, all muscle and thorn. A trunklike loop rose and ringed him. He lunged for his axe, but he had left it in the grass. Something rasped his boot, wrapped his ankle, and yanked him into a black throat under the spring. Panic emptied his chest. He kicked, but the weight on his leg was not iron; it was the lady herself, holding him. In the dim light her true shape swam clear: from her hips unfurled a scaled tail, flexing in smooth, merciless waves. She circled, swallowing water and blowing perfect globes of air, as if weighing his worth.

The coils closed. Armor buckled, ribs cracked, the borrowed steel that made him bold turned to a cage that cut him. He reached for breath and found only green. When she let go, he sank among old bones and rust. Above, she drifted, eyes half-closed, her golden tail’s tip writing lazy circles on the calm. Outside, the tower’s fire guttered; men drank; the garden kept its secret.
